speaker: interviewer
text: Bill, you mentioned that the-- the Nazis took away pictures from people. Did you have a picture they took away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had some pictures of the family, everything. Everything. I mean, I don't know whether they burned the clothes, whatever. But I have-- I had nothing, just one of those pajama type of striped outfits. And they didn't tattoo us, because-- later I found out why. Two days later, they called out and they asked for volunteers. Not volunteers, not really volunteers. But they asked for people that had worked on the railroads. I had no idea why. But while I was in this barrack for a couple days, there was people-- there-- some of the people, they were there for longer time said, kid, no matter what you do, no matter what they ask-- no matter what they ask of you to do, as long as they offer you work and you can get work, go and work. You don't work, you will die. They will kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they asked who worked on railroads, of course I raised my hands. Where did you work on railroads? In Budapest. Budapest, when I did forced labor camp-- forced labor. And I told them-- gave them the name of the slave driver that I worked for in Budapest for a period of time that was a government agency. And somehow they believed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know what our mission would be. So finally, they-- they gathered 500 of us and they marched us into a railroad station. And they put us into cattle cars, 48 in each cattle car. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name that you gave them? Do you remember the name of the person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No way I can remember. But what I do remember, that they announced that we will be now known as members of the 10th Eisenbahn railroad-- Eisenbahn-- 10th German Eisenbahn Brigade. Well, it sounds fancy. But what it really meant is that they formed-- as the Americans were approaching and they were constantly bombarding the railroads, they organized groups of 500 and trains that were constantly moving in every direction. So when the railroads were bombed, it was very easy to find one these brigades, as they called it, which consisted of 500 people, German guards, and equipment to fix the railroads. So this way they were able to maintain the railroads behind the line very quickly. I wind up on one of those, several days later on one of those work camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It sounds good in a way, because I was able to work, and they gave us food, just enough for survival. The problem was the following, that we never knew when they would take us and blow the whistle and we would have to start working. Day and night, we were constantly-- we didn't know where we were. We were never told where we were. But the routine was the following, that when we stopped somewheres, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans got out, set up the machine guns, and started screaming with their dogs, [GERMAN] out, out, out. And what we did is, they opened up a cattle car. We got out. And we started digging two foxholes alongside the railway. We stopped behind [INAUDIBLE] or behind the bombed-out railroad. But we dug foxholes all the way through, and under Germans watching us. Then we went back. And the Germans went into the foxholes and set up machine guns facing the railroad tracks. Second time we come out, they opened up the-- the places where they kept the equipment. We had all the equipment to fix the railroads. And we were sometimes day and night, 24 hours, as long as we fixed the railroads. Constantly being guarded by the Germans from both sides. If anyone made a move to try to escape, he was immediately shot. I mean, you just had to watch--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Do exactly-- what did you ask?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to sabotage the railroad or-- or not do their job?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not-- we-- we did not have the opportunity to do that. We-- I mean, we were so guarded, there's no way we-- we sabotage the railroad. But this was always-- they always brought us to railroads, because they had to-- this was always close to the lines. Quite often we would hear the-- the explosion of-- of the guns, because we were never too far from the front lines. And that's how the Americans bombed the railroads, not to bring up more ammunition to the front lines.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were constantly in danger to be shot by the Germans. And if we were injured, they took you on a little trolley with a pretence that they're taking you to sanitarium to get well. And then we just heard the machine guns go off. And we knew when the trolley came back an hour later-- we knew they never went to sanitarium. Sanitarium was they were executed.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, if we survived the Germans, then what happened, the Americans came by with reconnaissance planes to see whether the mission was successful. And it came down quite low. They dived low. And now here they see they are fixing the railroads that some flyers have risked their life to bomb. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, I can understand that. Their mission was to stop the movement, German movement, so they started machine-gunning the railroad. They low, low. And half of us were killed by Americans from the air. Whether they knew that we were Jewish prisoners, who we were, all they knew is that we are fixing the railroads that they just bombed. Whether I can justify it or not, that's for God to know. I don't know. But I can understand why they were not interested in a few hundred Holocaust survivors, or Holocaust [INAUDIBLE], slaves, whatever you call us. They were more interested in winning the war and that was their job, to stop it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, this went on for like-- from December 'til about March. By that time, everyone was killed. There were about 40 of us were left.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was under the supervision of the SS. The SS made a decision that they would take us in the woods and execute us, kill us. But there were also some Wehrmacht soldiers, which were not the front-line soldiers. They made a deal with SS that they would take over our group and marched them in. And it wasn't march us in into a camp. We didn't know what camp. But not because they were so good. But because they made a deal with us that we would testify that they saved our life. They knew-- they were open with-- the Wehrmacht soldiers were open with us, and they knew that the war is going to be lost. They said, you will have to testify for us, that we saved your life. Then we are going to march you in. So that's how the 40 of us-- SS for some reason, gave in. It was at that time already a feeling of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are losing the war. And the Wehrmacht started marching us into camp. And the camp where they brought us back in was Dachau, because we were not far from Munich. We had no idea where we were, because we were constantly traveling. And that march cost-- probably about 20% of people were killed during that march, for several reasons. And a couple of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] episodes that stand out and have haunted me for years and years and years and years. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's not a death march you're talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a death march, no. No. This was not a death march. It was not intended to be a death march. But one of them was that we were friends-- and some of them I've remembered from Budapest, because remember, this was all the Hungarian group that was brought in. Some of them I remember from Budapest and we befriended-- there were three of us. We were kids. We befriended each other. And we sort of pledged that we'll die for each other, if necessary, but we'll save each other's life. This is-- kids have this kind of dream, you know. We'll go to war and I will put my life on the line for you. Well, we said the same thing. One of us dies, we will rather all the three of us die. The test came when one of the boys had a crushed knee and it started becoming gangrene. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we marched him by holding up, holding him up. It was not easy, but we did whatever we could to save him. There was a group of SS. They constantly were coming by, going somewheres. And a truck stops by, checks out who we are, where are going, and so forth. And this one German just goes by, looks at everybody, and looks at this kid's face. And says, let him go. Well, here, we took it seriously as kids that we will sacrifice our life. We make pledges like that in our life, and we think we can keep them. But when it comes to the reality, it isn't the same as what we believe we will do. He-- we didn't. Another minute goes by and we didn't. He puts the Luger, puts it against my forehead, and says, you drop him by the count of three or I shoot you, and through your head I shoot him. I dropped him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had-- even though I was a kid, it was haunting me. For years I had nightmares of that that I didn't save him. I mean, we swore we would save each other. I didn't. It was a question of I die or we both die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure--

speaker: interviewer
text: --choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But it doesn't stop you from haunting you. It's like being in Vietnam and two buddies decide they will save each other. And the Vietnam soldier could come up and say, you drop him, I kill him, you can live, and he drops him. That-- that-- I mean, it drives you mad. This was driving me mad for years. I had nightmares on that. It was really one-- one of those horrible experiences that-- that haunted me for years. But I'm past it now. But I've learned one thing, that we can never say never, I'll never do this or I'll never do that. When we are faced with death, we do what we have to survive. And there were several other occasions like that. Yet, we had an interesting experience. And this is why I have learned not to condemn the world or not even all the Germans, the guilty ones, because there was a woman, as we were marched through a small village. She stood on the second floor. I could see her face now, an elderly woman, wrinkled face. And she had an apron up and stood against the window, which was open, and looked at us and looked. And as we passed under the window, she dropped the apron. And about 20 loaves of bread just rolled out from the window onto the floor, right on top of us. Well, of course, we jumped on top of each other and we devoured it and-- but the SS soldier was standing behind the corner on guard duty, aimed his machine shoe gun, shot her, and we saw her fall back. Killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, later on, think about the-- the awesome decision that one has to make to hate everybody, hate every German. Yet this woman gave her life for us in the worst time. So how do you hate every German? There were some. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's-- it was very, very difficult to-- to learn to cope with what is real and what is not, what is just, what is unjust. No matter-- I mean, if somebody's guilty of course I could execute him myself. But the whole country, the whole nation? So, eventually we came in into Dachau. What city was that in, where the woman dropped the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was somewheres between Munich and Dachau. Dachau is about 40, 50 kilometers from Munich. And it was somewheres-- I knew it was a small community. I never knew which community we're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've had several, several incidences which were just as horrible. But these two have remained with me the rest of my life, because one was a question of my pledge, which I couldn't keep. Another one was, do I condemn the rest of my life when this happened to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then eventually we wound up and we came in to Dachau, somewheres the third week of April or so. And in Dachau, the Germans were already approaching Munich at that time. We didn't know that they have heard about Dachau and they're preparing to liberate, which was the first camp that Americans liberated under Eisenhower. But the Germans wanted to destroy the whole camp. But one thing we did see, that they were wiring up the whole camp to explode it. They were preparing to explode the whole camp. There were 29,000 people in Dachau. But we all had lice. We were all filthy. We all slept four and five of us in one bin. And we-- if one had lice, everybody had it the next day. And the 29th of April, early in the morning we heard gunshots and explosions. And for a moment we thought, there we go. The camp is being exploded. But that's not what happened. The Americans marched in, totally unexpected, and caught them off guard completely, the Germans. Then the scene that-- that followed was unbelievable, because people literally tore some of the Germans apart. I-- we lost complete contact of this 40, of this group of Wehrmacht people that brought us into Dachau. I have no idea what happened to them, and neither did I care.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was the day that we were liberated in Dachau. There were several stories that happened. Maybe one of the stories I will-- I will tell, just to explain that what we-- would you ever believe, for example, that I would be involved in cannibalism, as you know me? You see, I would rather die.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the system was, in Dachau, the old-timers, they know how to deal and wheel. And one of the most valuable commodity was potato chips, this potato peels. So we get a hold, several of us, of potato peels. And of the guys says, I know somebody who's got a hold on a cattle. And he killed a cattle and he has got beef. And then he can change it. This slice of beef, it's roasted somehow, but it'll cost you a certain amount of potato peels. We made a trade. And a group of us participated in doing that. Later on we found that there was no cattle. It was human flesh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know at the time, or we probably would not have devoured it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later on, I said, my god. Did I literally participate in cannibalism? But yes. Anything, anything, that's when I meant-- I mean-- Even if you suspect that subconsciously a little bit, but you still-- Now matter how moral you wanted to remain, you couldn't remain that moral that you would rather give your life than break your moral code.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those are the people mostly that survived. Mostly it was that survival was above everything. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So are-- are you saying the people that survived were the ones who were able to somehow break the moral code?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I will admit to you that we slept in bins, four of us with one blanket. We went to bed, and there was one blanket covering, and it was freezing cold. It was-- it was early spring, but it was cold, terribly cold. And woke up on many occasions where someone next to me didn't warm me. And when I started shaking him, he was dead. It didn't move me. There was no cry. There no emotion. All I ask is the guy next to me, let's take him off and dump him down on the floor. Now we have a blanket, the three of us, and we can survive another night. If I had become morally involved, emotionally involved in this person, I would have not made it. You had to-- you-- I didn't kill anyone, but neither did I cry for anybody in camp. Different camps had different situations. This was my situation. So that's what I meant, the will to live. That he died was not as painful to me as the fact-- was not painful to me, really. It was almost a relief, that now we can have three of us a blanket. Now we can find his piece of bread that he didn't eat and divide it between us. There was no other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel guilty about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some things. Most things not. Most things not, because had I not had the will power to survive-- I meant you had luck and willpower. Had I not have, I wouldn't be here talking to you. So how can I say I feel sorry, feel sorry for surviving? Yes, I felt guilty at the beginning, why I survived, not my parents, not my sisters and brothers. Today, no. Today I have a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today I'm more interested in what can I do to make the world-- to leave the world a better world after I'm gone? But not-- I don't waste that much time about the guilt of the camps. Not anymore. It is what I can create now 'til the time that God will give me. It's more important. After the war, how did people cope with having broken the moral code? How did it affect their life after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some went completely crazy. We had one person-- some-- some killed themselves. I would like to tell you just one story that will take about three, four minutes-- and I'm not sure whether we have enough tape at this time-- between the time that I was liberated and the time I went home. It's quite an interesting story. And then I will describe to you what has happened to some people when they could not cope when they came home, how they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: If-- if--

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you saw them or knew them personally--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a cousin of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because we're interested in your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a cousin of mine. Well, the first story--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first story was mine, that because we had lice, we had typhoid fever, which, you know, so many people died after especially Dachau. So many people died after liberation. And about a week or so-- now, we were liberated April the 9th-- the 29th. And about a week later, I just collapsed on the street and I passed out. I remember no more. I don't remember anything after that. And the only thing that I remember that happened to me, that I woke up sometimes, somewheres, where I saw as if I were on clouds and everything is-- is white. And whatever I see, a white-- white room, everything's just a white-- I could not distinguish what it was. But in my mind, subconsciously, somehow, that this must be death-like. I'm floating on a cloud. I must be dead. Now, this has been already 10, 15 days that has gone by since I collapsed. And I moved and a German nurse passed by and she screamed, Herr Doctor! [GERMAN] I mean, Doctor, come quickly. And Doctor came over to me, and I passed out again. I don't remember what has happened afterwards. The next thing I remember, that I woke up and I-- my eyes were blur, completely blurred. And I didn't know where I am. But I saw like a huge sunburst, which started coming into focus slowly, slowly, slowly. And I realize that I'm somewheres. And the sunburst got smaller. And I realize this is a carnation I'm looking at. And there's a nurse sitting next to me. And she says, [GERMAN]. Happy birthday. It was June 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: So between that time, I was all the time in a coma. And because I moved just in the right time when a nurse passed by, then the nurse explained me what this room was. When they gave up all hope-- it was American field hospital. But there was no hope for these people to survive. I was thin. I was a skeleton.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave up feeding me, because there was no place to give, get in a vein or anything. So they laid them out on tables, covered them up with white sheets, to wait for the heart beats, and then they buried them. I didn't die. Some reason I was supposed to, but I didn't. And I just moved in the right time. And that's when they realized it, that if I survived so long the coma, they brought me back into field hospital, and they revived me. But it took another few weeks. And that's how between the second week-- no. April, May the 10th, approximately or so, to June 4th, I was in a coma. Bald hair, lost all my hair. But I made it. That's when I came to. June 4th.

speaker: interviewee
text: So how many lice I have? I don't know. But this is just one more incident that I survived. When did you decided to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now what happened, I came back to Budapest to search for my family. I couldn't find anyone for about a week. Finally, I discovered someone has seen my sister in Bucharest. My sister heard that I'm looking for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bucharest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bucharest, because there was-- this was the system. If you went either to Bucharest or to Budapest, the agencies, Jewish agencies, organized themselves, those that survived being deported, the wealthier Jews, and immediately started assisting those people that came back from camp. So my sister went there to get a certain amount of money. It was the equivalent of $250, something like that, to start life again. I went to Budapest. Then when I started going towards Bucharest to search for my sister, I heard that she is coming to Budapest to look for me. That time, I came home to my little small town in Czechoslovakia, to Sasovo. And I left posters and notices in the railroads and notices with anybody, whoever sees my sister to tell her that I am at home. I am not leaving home, 'til she doesn't come home, because we'll be chasing each other forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got off the railroad, two Jewish kids grabbed me and they brought me into my distant relative's house. And he says, you're not going into your own house. Why am I not going to my home? He said, because everyone who tried to recapture their houses was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: In your house there are six, seven Gentile families, peasants, that their homes were bombed out. You'll be dead tomorrow morning if you try to get back your house. So they brought-- brings me into a huge house. And what happened, the house was full of bedding, grain, food, no furniture, with these instructions. A distant relative of mine, who was much older than I am, came home, was very wealthy and was able to recapture a lot of beds, beddings, and grainery and food, and left a note that any Jewish kid that survives, comes home, should not go back to their home, but should stay together to watch each other from being killed. I knew this. After surviving, in my village I must be afraid to be killed. And after he did this, he left a note that this is his last contribution to the Holocaust, to those that survived the Holocaust, and he hung himself. He committed suicide. He says, I cannot cope with what I've experienced. I cannot go on, but this is my last contribution.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you asked me how some people coped, everybody differently. This man killed himself, but he left that behind. So I was there for several days, 'til finally my sister came home. And then we started taking off, getting away from Budapest. What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewheres.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, seeing her? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was joy, of course. It was joy. Not knowing whether anybody else survived. But we waited something like three or four weeks. And when nobody showed up, we assumed that everybody's dead. We didn't know about my brother Ted. We didn't know that he survived 'til we didn't get into Italy. This was like a year later, almost.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the two of us took off. We were in Austria. And we had experiences in the displaced persons camp. They were not the horrible experiences we had in concentration camp. But they were also very, very sad experiences and very difficult ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: But finally, we wound up in Italy, in a displaced person camp near Rome in a city called Grottaferrata. And there we organized ourselves under the [INAUDIBLE] flag, because we were always revisionist-- as I said, I stumble on that word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Revisionist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Revisionist movement, always belonged to that movement. So we formed a kibbutz type of life. But it was not like in Israel, because we didn't produce anything. But we were shared everything. Everything that anybody had belonged to everybody else. And we were awaiting to get into Israel.
